era_context: The late 10th century CE was a period of dynastic succession and regional consolidation across Eurasia. In Europe, the Holy Roman Empire under Otto II was declining, and the Byzantine Empire was entering a period of military expansion under Basil II. In East Asia, the Northern Song Dynasty had just unified much of China under Taizu, who died in 976 CE, succeeded by his brother Taizong. The Islamic world remained fragmented among competing caliphates. Scandinavia and Eastern Europe were in the process of Christianization. The year 976 CE marks a significant turning point in both European and East Asian history.

events.title: Death of Emperor Taizu of Song; succession of Taizong
events.description: Emperor Taizu of Song (r. 960–976) died on 27 November 976 CE, succeeded by his brother Taizong (r. 976–997). Taizu had founded the Song Dynasty and unified much of China, establishing the capital at Kaifeng and implementing administrative reforms. His death marked the transition to Taizong's reign, which would continue and expand upon Taizu's policies of centralization and civil service reform. The succession was smooth and legitimate, establishing a precedent for orderly imperial succession in the Song Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Taizu's death on 27 November 976 CE is confirmed by multiple Chinese historical sources. The date and succession are well-attested.

events.title: Basil II accedes to the Byzantine throne
events.description: Emperor Basil II Bulgaroktonos (r. 976–1025) acceded to the Byzantine throne in 976 CE following the death of his father John I Tzimiskes (r. 969–976). Basil II was approximately 18 years old at his accession and initially shared power with his brother Constantine VIII. Basil II's reign would be marked by military expansion, particularly in the Balkans and against the Fatimid Caliphate, and by administrative reforms. His accession marked the beginning of a period of Byzantine military and cultural flourishing.

sources.name: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetus, De Administrando Imperio

events.certainty_note: Basil II's accession in 976 CE is confirmed by Byzantine chronicles and contemporary sources. The date and succession are well-attested.

events.title: Al-Aziz bi-Allah accedes to the Fatimid Caliphate
events.description: Caliph Al-Aziz bi-Allah (r. 975–996) acceded to the Fatimid Caliphate in 975 CE (not 976, but included here for chronological context). However, 976 CE marks the consolidation of his power and the beginning of his major military and administrative initiatives. Al-Aziz was establishing Fatimid control over Egypt and the Levant, strengthening the Mamluk military forces, and expanding Fatimid naval power. His reign marked a period of Fatimid cultural and economic prosperity.
events.certainty_note: Al-Aziz's accession occurred in 975 CE, not 976 CE. However, 976 CE marks the consolidation of his power. The dating is based on Arabic chronicles, which are sometimes less precise than contemporary Western sources.

events.title: Otto II's reign continues amid internal instability in the Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Emperor Otto II (r. 973–983) was ruling the Holy Roman Empire in 976 CE, but his authority was increasingly challenged by rebellious German princes and his southern Italian campaigns were failing. In 976 CE, Otto II was facing internal opposition and military setbacks that would eventually lead to his death in 983 CE. His reign marked the beginning of the decline of Ottonian centralization and the fragmentation of imperial authority.
events.certainty_note: Otto II's reign and the internal instability of the Holy Roman Empire are attested in German chronicles, but specific events of 976 CE are not precisely dated.

events.title: Christianization of Poland under Mieszko I
events.description: Duke Mieszko I of Poland (r. 960–992) had adopted Christianity around 966 CE, and by 976 CE the process of Christianization was well underway. In 976 CE, the integration of Poland into the Latin Christian world was progressing, with the establishment of churches and the adoption of Christian practices by the Polish elite. The Christianization of Poland was both a religious and political process, aligning Poland with Western European powers.
events.certainty_note: The Christianization of Poland is attested in German and Polish chronicles, but the process was gradual and specific events of 976 CE are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The succession of Taizong to the Song throne is sometimes presented as uncontroversial, but some sources suggest there may have been questions about the legitimacy of the succession, particularly regarding whether Taizu's young son should have been passed over. However, modern scholarship generally accepts the succession as legitimate and orderly. Basil II's accession is sometimes portrayed as the beginning of a period of uninterrupted military success, but his early reign was marked by internal opposition and military setbacks, particularly against the Fatimid Caliphate. Otto II's reign is sometimes presented as a straightforward decline, but recent scholarship emphasizes the complexity of his political situation and the rational basis for some of his military decisions.
historiographic_note: The year 976 CE is well-documented for East Asia, with extensive Chinese historical sources confirming Taizu's death and Taizong's succession. Byzantine sources are also available for Basil II's accession. Western European sources are less precise but confirm Otto II's continued reign and internal instability. Islamic sources are available for Al-Aziz's consolidation of power. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are less precisely documented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are essentially absent from the written record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Taizu's death in 976 led to Taizong's reign, which lasted until 997
Basil II's accession in 976 began his reign, which lasted until 1025
Al-Aziz's consolidation in 976 was part of his reign until 996
Otto II's internal instability in 976 culminated in his death in 983
Christianization of Poland continued through Mieszko I's reign until his death in 992
